claim: The Currier A/B split corresponds to two different scribal hands applying the same underlying cipher to the same language but with different glyph allograph preferences, predictable via bigram transition matrix divergence between A and B corpora exceeding random variation.
evidence_for: Currier B is ~2.15x larger (23,766 vs 11,022 words), consistent with a dominant scribe and a secondary contributor
Both A and B share top vocabulary items (daiin, ol, chedy), suggesting shared cipher not separate languages
Glyph entropy difference between sections is small (< 0.15 bits), arguing against radically different encoding schemes
evidence_against: H004/H008 at 0.88 confidence argue genuine linguistic difference
Some folios show mixed A/B features, complicating a clean two-scribe split
test: Compute bigram transition probability matrices P_A and P_B for Currier A and B corpora separately, then measure Jensen-Shannon divergence JS(P_A || P_B). Compare to JS divergence computed between random same-size splits of the combined corpus (bootstrap n=1000).
test_metric: Jensen-Shannon divergence of bigram matrices between A and B vs. null distribution from random splits
test_threshold: If JS(A,B) < 95th percentile of null distribution, scribal allograph variation explains split; if JS(A,B) > 99th percentile, genuine encoding or language difference is indicated

tests_run.test: compute bigram transition probability matrices p_a and p_b for currier a and b corpora separately, t
tests_run.details: Language A: entropy=3.8321, 3513 unique/11022 total words. Language B: entropy=3.8611, 5089 unique/23766 total words. Entropy difference: 0.0290. Vocabulary density difference: 0.1046.

reasoning: If allograph variation alone explains A/B, the solution space collapses to a single language+cipher; if not, we must model two encoding streams, doubling complexity. This test directly arbitrates between the two highest-confidence competing hypotheses.
